topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Counting two ways
prompt: Let $p$ be a positive integer, $p > 1$. Find the number of $m \times n$ matrices with entries in the set $\{1,2,3,..., p\}$ and such that the sum of elements on each row and each column is not divisible by $p$.
response_text:
Let the entries be considered modulo \(p\). We need the number of \(m\times n\) matrices over \(\{1,2,\dots,p\}\) such that every row sum and every column sum is nonzero modulo \(p\).

Use the roots-of-unity filter. Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\). For an integer \(s\),
\[
\mathbf 1_{s\not\equiv 0\pmod p}
=\frac1p\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\omega^{ks}.
\]
So the desired count is
\[
N=\sum_{A}\prod_{i=1}^m \mathbf 1_{\text{row}_i(A)\not\equiv 0}\prod_{j=1}^n \mathbf 1_{\text{col}_j(A)\not\equiv 0}.
\]
Substituting the filter gives
\[
N=\frac1{p^{m+n}}
\sum_{\substack{a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb Z_p\\ a_i\ne 0}}
\sum_{\substack{b_1,\dots,b_n\in\mathbb Z_p\\ b_j\ne 0}}
\sum_{A}\omega^{\sum_{i,j}(a_i+b_j)A_{ij}}.
\]
For a fixed choice of \((a_i),(b_j)\), the sum over each entry \(A_{ij}\in\{1,\dots,p\}\) is
\[
\sum_{x=1}^p \omega^{(a_i+b_j)x}
=
\begin{cases}
p,& a_i+b_j\equiv 0\pmod p,\\
0,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence the whole contribution is nonzero iff \(a_i+b_j\equiv 0\) for all \(i,j\). That forces all \(a_i\) to be equal to some nonzero \(a\), and all \(b_j\) to equal \(-a\). There are \(p-1\) such choices, and then every entry contributes a factor \(p\), giving \(p^{mn}\).

Therefore
\[
N=\frac{(p-1)p^{mn}}{p^{m+n}}=(p-1)p^{mn-m-n}.
\]

So the number of such matrices is

\[
\boxed{(p-1)p^{mn-m-n}}.
\]

Final answer: \((p-1)p^{mn-m-n}\)
